title: Why Giant Dragonflies Disappeared: A New Discovery
summary: Long ago, giant dragonflies with wings as wide as a ruler flew in the sky. Scientists thought they needed more oxygen in the air to breathe because insects have tiny tubes for breathing, not lungs like us. As oxygen levels dropped, the big bugs couldn't survive anymore. But a new study shows this idea might be wrong. Researchers looked at many insects, from tiny ones to big beetles, and took pictures of their muscles. They found that the breathing tubes inside the muscles didn't take up much more space in bigger insects. This means the old explanation about oxygen might not be correct, and we still need to learn why giant insects are gone today.

questions.question: Is the old explanation about oxygen correct, based on the summary?

background_read: Long ago, during a time called the late Palaeozoic era, the Earth had different air with more oxygen. Giant insects like dragonflies with huge wings lived then. Today, insects are much smaller. Scientists have been trying to figure out why these big bugs disappeared. They thought it was because the air changed and had less oxygen, making it hard for big insects to breathe. But new research is showing this might not be the whole story, and we need to keep exploring to understand the mystery.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists and researchers studying ancient giant insects
WHAT: A study that challenges the idea that giant insects needed more oxygen to breathe
WHERE: In the skies long ago and in modern labs
WHY: To learn why giant dragonflies are no longer around today

perspectives.description: Scientists thought giant insects disappeared because oxygen levels dropped, making it hard for them to breathe.
Recent research suggests breathing tubes in insects don't take up much more space in bigger ones, so oxygen might not be the main reason.
